title: Homepage Redesign Sprint Planning
description: Define sprint goals, assign owners, and align homepage redesign priorities across UX, frontend, and content teams.
paragraphs: The homepage redesign sprint planning session was held on June 7, 2026, and lasted two hours. Alice facilitated the meeting while Bob reviewed the existing homepage analytics and user feedback collected during the previous sprint.

The team agreed that the main goal of this sprint is to improve first-time visitor clarity. The hero section will be rewritten, the primary call-to-action will be made more visible, and the navigation labels will be simplified.

User research showed that new visitors often struggled to understand the product value within the first ten seconds. The new design will introduce a shorter headline, a supporting subheading, and a stronger visual hierarchy above the fold.

Action items: Alice will finalize the sprint backlog, Bob will prepare the homepage wireframe updates, and the frontend team will create reusable layout components for the hero, feature highlights, and testimonial sections.
The sprint follows a two-week Agile cycle. Daily standups will continue at 10:00 AM, design review will happen every Wednesday, and the sprint retrospective is scheduled for June 19, 2026.
Key risks include delayed copy approval, inconsistent spacing across responsive breakpoints, and possible conflicts between marketing goals and usability recommendations.
The team agreed to measure success using homepage bounce rate, click-through rate on the main CTA, and average time spent on the landing page after deployment.
The final homepage redesign should be ready for internal review before June 10, 2026.

title: Website Navigation IA Review
description: Review and restructure the website navigation to improve discoverability and reduce user confusion.
paragraphs: The information architecture review started on June 8, 2026. Bob is leading the task, with Alice reviewing the UX research notes and customer journey maps.

The current navigation contains too many top-level items, which makes the website feel more complex than necessary. The team proposed reducing the main navigation to five primary sections: Product, Solutions, Pricing, Resources, and Contact.
A card sorting exercise will be used to validate whether users understand the new grouping. The test will include at least eight participants from the target user group.
Frontend implementation will require updates to the header component, mobile menu drawer, active route styling, and keyboard accessibility states.
The mobile navigation needs special attention because analytics show that more than half of website visits come from mobile devices.
Acceptance criteria include clear hover states, accessible focus indicators, correct route highlighting, and no layout shift when the menu opens or closes.
The navigation update will be reviewed during the next sprint demo, and feedback will be added to the backlog before development is finalized.
Open question: whether the Resources section should include Blog, Case Studies, and Documentation under one dropdown or as separate landing pages.

title: Design System Button Components
description: Create reusable button components for primary, secondary, danger, and ghost actions across the website.
paragraphs: This task focuses on building a consistent button component system for the website. Alice created the initial design notes, and Bob will help confirm frontend implementation requirements.
The website currently uses inconsistent button spacing, border radius, hover effects, and font weights across different pages. This creates a less polished user experience.
The new component should support primary, secondary, danger, ghost, and disabled states. Each variant should work in both desktop and mobile layouts.
Accessibility requirements include visible focus outlines, sufficient color contrast, semantic button usage, and support for keyboard navigation.
The component should accept common props such as variant, size, loading, disabled, icon position, and click handler.
Testing should cover visual consistency, loading state behavior, disabled state behavior, and responsive alignment inside different layout containers.
The button component will be added to the shared component folder and documented in the internal design system page.
This task is planned for the next sprint after the homepage redesign reaches internal review.

title: Pricing Page UX Improvement
description: Improve the pricing page layout, plan comparison table, and conversion-focused content.
paragraphs: The pricing page improvement task was created after reviewing user feedback from the last release. Several users reported that plan differences were not clear enough.
Bob is responsible for restructuring the comparison table and making the recommended plan visually easier to identify. Alice will review the final layout before implementation.
The new pricing page will include three plan cards, a clearer monthly versus annual billing toggle, and a feature comparison section below the fold.
The team agreed to avoid overwhelming users with too many technical details at the top of the page. Advanced limits and enterprise features will be moved into an expandable section.
Success metrics include improved pricing page CTA clicks, fewer support questions about plan differences, and increased trial conversion rate.
Frontend risks include maintaining table readability on mobile screens and ensuring the billing toggle does not cause confusing layout shifts.
This task will be developed behind a feature flag so that the team can preview the new page internally before release.
The first design review is scheduled for June 24, 2026, and implementation should be completed before July 2, 2026.

title: Responsive Layout QA Sprint
description: Run responsive design QA across key website pages and fix layout issues for tablet and mobile screens.
paragraphs: The responsive layout QA sprint will cover the homepage, pricing page, contact page, blog listing page, and product feature pages.
Alice will prepare the QA checklist, while Bob will confirm the target breakpoints and browser coverage before testing begins.
The main breakpoints are mobile, tablet, small laptop, and desktop. The team will manually test common viewport widths and record layout defects in the project board.
Known areas of concern include overflowing text in cards, inconsistent section padding, large images pushing content below the fold, and mobile menu spacing.
Each issue should include a screenshot, browser name, viewport size, expected behavior, and actual behavior.
The team will prioritize fixes based on user impact. Critical issues that block reading or navigation will be handled before minor visual inconsistencies.
Acceptance criteria include no horizontal scrolling, readable typography, clear CTA placement, and consistent spacing between sections.
The QA sprint is planned after the pricing page update so the team can test the website more holistically.

title: Website Accessibility Audit
description: Audit core website pages for accessibility issues and create a prioritized improvement backlog.
paragraphs: The accessibility audit will focus on improving keyboard navigation, color contrast, semantic HTML, ARIA usage, and form accessibility.
Bob will lead the technical audit, and Alice will review design-level accessibility issues such as color contrast, focus states, and readable font sizes.
The audit will cover the homepage, pricing page, login page, contact form, navigation menu, and footer links.
The team will use automated tools as a starting point, but manual testing is also required because automated checks cannot catch every accessibility issue.
Keyboard testing should confirm that users can navigate the header, dropdown menus, forms, buttons, and modal dialogs without using a mouse.
Form fields must have clear labels, helpful error messages, and visible validation states. Error messages should explain what went wrong and how to fix it.
The output of this task will be a prioritized backlog with severity levels: critical, high, normal, and low.
The team agreed that accessibility improvements should be treated as part of normal quality work, not as optional polish after release.

title: Website Content Review Workflow
description: Define an Agile workflow for reviewing, approving, and publishing website content updates.
paragraphs: The content review workflow was discussed during the June 9 sprint refinement session. Alice documented the proposed process and shared it with the product and marketing teams.
The previous workflow was informal, which caused delays when page copy required approval from multiple stakeholders.
The new workflow includes four stages: draft, design review, product approval, and ready for development.
Each content item must have one owner, one reviewer, and one final approver. This avoids confusion when multiple people leave comments on the same copy document.
For Agile tracking, content tasks will be added to the same project board as design and development tasks. This makes dependencies easier to see during sprint planning.
Copy changes that affect layout must be reviewed by the frontend team before development starts.
The team agreed that small typo fixes can be handled as quick tasks, but major messaging changes should go through the full review process.
The workflow was approved on June 12, 2026, and will be used for all website content updates starting from the next sprint.

title: Contact Form Redesign & Validation
description: Redesign the contact form and improve client-side validation, error messages, and submission feedback.
paragraphs: The contact form redesign task was added after support reported that some users were submitting incomplete or unclear inquiries.
Bob is implementing the frontend validation logic, while Alice is reviewing the updated form layout and microcopy.
The redesigned form will include fields for name, email, company, inquiry type, message, and consent confirmation.
Validation should run before submission and provide clear inline error messages. Required fields must be marked clearly without relying only on color.
The form should display a loading state during submission and a success confirmation after the request is sent.
Error handling should cover invalid email format, missing required fields, network failure, and server-side validation errors.
The team will also review whether inquiry type should be a dropdown, radio group, or segmented control based on usability and mobile layout.
This task is expected to improve form completion quality and reduce back-and-forth clarification emails from the support team.
